summary: King of Wessex (r. 871-899) and the only English monarch ever given the epithet "the Great," identified in giveaways as the ruler whose biography was written by the Welsh monk Bishop Asser. His signature achievement was defeating Guthrum's Viking army at the Battle of Edington, which led to the Treaty of Wedmore establishing the Danelaw; he also built a defensive network of fortified towns (burhs) recorded in the Burghal Hidage, compiled the law code known as the Doom Book, and sponsored Old English translations of Latin texts including Gregory the Great's Pastoral Care and Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Alfred is identified in giveaways above all as the only English king or monarch ever to carry the epithet "the Great," ruling as King of Wessex and, after uniting Wessex with English-held Mercia, the first to style himself King of the Anglo-Saxons. His Old English name is traditionally glossed as meaning "wise elf."

clues.clue: The only English king or monarch ever given the epithet "the Great"
examples: Guthrum was defeated by this king of Wessex. This king is the only English monarch with the epithet "great."
This late-9th century king of Wessex is the only English king to be dignified with the epithet 'the Great' for his success in stemming the Viking onslaught.

clues.clue: His name is traditionally glossed as meaning "wise elf"
examples: With name meaning "wise elf," this ruler fought against Ivar the Boneless, won a major victory at the Battle of Edington against Guthrum the Old.

clues.clue: First West Saxon king to style himself "King of the Anglo-Saxons" after uniting Wessex with English Mercia
examples: Aethelflaed was the daughter of this king of Wessex, who was the first to call himself king of the Anglo-Saxons.
This first king of the Anglo-Saxons had the epithet "the Great."

works.name: Battle of Edington
works.description: Alfred's decisive 878 victory over Guthrum's Viking army at the Battle of Edington is his most-clued single achievement, reversing a war that had earlier forced him into hiding at a fortress on the Isle of Athelney. The battle directly produced the Treaty of Wedmore and ended the immediate threat of the Great Heathen Army to Wessex.

clues.clue: Decisively defeated Guthrum's Viking army (part of the Great Heathen Army) at the Battle of Edington, his defining military victory
examples: The Viking army was led by Guthrum when it was defeated by this man at the Battle of Edington.
For 10 points, name his King of Wessex best known for fending off Guthrum's Great Heathen Army.

clues.clue: Hid out at a fortress on the Isle of Athelney, toured by his biographer Bishop Asser, before rallying his army to victory
examples: This man's life was chronicled by the Bishop Asser, who toured the fortress on the Isle of Athelney where this man hid out before leading his army to victory at the Battle of Edington.

clues.clue: His opponent at Edington, Guthrum the Old, was linked to the Viking commander Ivar the Boneless
examples: With name meaning "wise elf," this ruler fought against Ivar the Boneless, won a major victory at the Battle of Edington against Guthrum the Old.

works.name: Treaty of Wedmore
works.description: The Treaty of Wedmore, Alfred's peace settlement with Guthrum following Edington, established the Danelaw and required Guthrum's conversion to Christianity. Its terms and circumstances were clued heavily on their own as a distinct answerline, separate from Alfred's biographical giveaways.

clues.clue: Established the Danelaw, drawing a boundary that ran along Watling Street
examples: One of the borders set at this treaty was at Watling Street, and this agreement was signed as a result of the Battle of Edington.
the Danelaw was established by what treaty signed between a Viking ruler and the only English king to be called "the Great"?

clues.clue: Guthrum was baptized (taking the Christian name Aethelstan) and adopted as Alfred's godson as a condition of the peace
examples: Before the signing of this treaty, one leader allegedly was baptised, took the name Aethelstan, and was taken as an adopted son by the other.
He adopted his former enemy Guthrum and forced his conversion to Christianity.

clues.clue: Set compensation for unlawful killing at eight half-marks of gold to discourage private violence
examples: It set the compensation for unlawful killings at eight half-marks of gold in an attempt to discourage further private violence.

clues.clue: One boundary point named was a fortress called "Cairceri" in the kingdom of the Hwicce
examples: This agreement resulted in the promised departure of one side to a fortress with the Welsh name "Cairceri" in the kingdom of the Hwicce.

clues.clue: Divided the kingdom of Mercia between Alfred and Guthrum
examples: This "great" King of Wessex divided Mercia with Guthrum by signing the Treaty of Wedmore.

clues.clue: The Peace of Wedmore and a separate agreement at Chippenham appear conflated with this treaty's signing in the fifty-sixth chapter of Asser's biography
examples: The Peace of Wedmore and agreement at Chippenham appears to have been conflated with the signing of this document in the fifty-sixth chapter of Asser.

works.name: Battle of Ashdown
works.description: Before becoming king, Alfred fought at the Battle of Ashdown (871) against an earlier Viking force, an episode remembered for the legend of the "Blowing Stone" used to rally troops and the death of the Viking leader Bagsecg.

clues.clue: Fought at the Battle of Ashdown before becoming king, allegedly summoning troops with the "Blowing Stone"; the Viking leader Bagsecg and his five earls were killed there
examples: Before becoming king, Alfred won this battle after allegedly summing troops with the Blowing Stone. Bagsecg and his five earls all died here, but the invasion of The Great Heathen Army wasn't ended until the Battle of Edington seven years later.
This king of Wessex defeated the Danes at the Battle of Ashdown, created a network of fortresses called Burhs, and translated Boethius' Consolation of Philosophy.

works.name: Doom Book
works.description: Alfred compiled a law code known as the Doom Book (Domboc), which he explicitly built upon the earlier codes of Aethelberht of Kent and Offa of Mercia rather than writing from scratch.

clues.clue: Compiled the Doom Book, a law code that built upon the earlier codes of Aethelberht of Kent and Offa of Mercia
examples: He built upon the law codes of Aethelberht of Kent and Offa of Mercia in the Doom Book.
Won a major victory at the Battle of Edington against Guthrum the Old and issued the Doom Book.

works.name: Burhs & Military Reforms
works.description: Alfred built a network of fortified towns called burhs across Wessex to defend against Viking raids, a system recorded in the Burghal Hidage; the network was extended after his death by his daughter Aethelflaed and son Edward the Elder.

clues.clue: Built a network of fortified towns (burhs) to defend Wessex from Viking attack, recorded in the Burghal Hidage, which detailed thirty-plus fortresses each garrisoned by four men per "wall perch"
examples: A list of fortresses constructed under this ruler's authority is known as the Burghal Hidage.
The second "knot" was to construct and maintain burhs, or fortifications, which this king first systematically built in the late 9th century to protect Wessex from Viking invasion.

clues.clue: The burh system was extended after his death by his daughter Aethelflaed, "Lady of the Mercians," working with his son Edward the Elder
examples: A daughter of this ruler extended a defense system in which thirty fortresses were garrisoned by four men to each wall perch; that system of burhs was detailed in the Burghal Hidage during the reign of this ruler's son, Edward the Elder.

works.name: Educational & Literary Translations
works.description: Alfred sponsored a program of Old English translations of Latin texts he deemed "most necessary for all men to know," including Gregory the Great's Pastoral Care and Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy, and is linked to the compilation of the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle and a body of attributed proverbs.

clues.clue: Translated (or sponsored the translation of) Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy into Old English, describing in its preface his method as working "sometimes word for word, sometimes sense for sense"
examples: In a preface, this translator claimed to have worked "sometimes word for word, sometimes sense for sense" to translate The Consolation of Philosophy.
This king is traditionally credited with the translation of The Old English Boethius.

clues.clue: Translated Pope Gregory the Great's Pastoral Care (Regula Pastoralis), prefacing it with a call to translate texts "most necessary for all men to know"
examples: This man is credited with translations of texts "most necessary for all men to know," including a rendition of Pastoral Care by Gregory the Great.
This man translated the Regula Patoralis of Pope Gregory I and used his prologue to encourage education in the English language.

clues.clue: The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle was first compiled during his reign
examples: The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle was originally compiled during the reign of this ruler, who oversaw the baptism of Guthrum after the Battle of Edington.

clues.clue: Marie de France claimed her Anglo-Norman Aesop's Fables translations were sourced from an Old English Aesop version attributed to him
examples: Marie de France claimed that this author's Aesop translations were the source of her Fables.
Marie also published a set of Anglo-Norman translations of Aesop's Fables, which she translated from this author's Old English version.

clues.clue: Credited with a translation of Orosius's history incorporating the Norwegian traveler Ohthere's firsthand account of walrus hunting
examples: This translator was once credited with incorporating Ohthere's stories about hunting walruses into a rendition of the Orosius.

clues.clue: Attributed apocryphal wise sayings, the "Proverbs of Alfred," quoted in the Middle English poem The Owl and the Nightingale
examples: This "wise" person's apocryphal "proverbs" are quoted throughout The Owl and the Nightingale.
This man is attributed numerous sayings in The Owl and the Nightingale and his namesake 12th-century Proverbs.

works.name: Legacy & Legends
works.description: Alfred's main medieval biography, written by the Welsh monk Bishop Asser, is the source for most of his life story; later folklore added the famous cake-burning legend that isn't actually in Asser's text, and objects like the Alfred Jewel and stories about his health and family survive as further biographical detail.

clues.clue: His main medieval biography, the Life of King Alfred, was written by the Welsh monk Bishop Asser
examples: For 10 points, the Welsh monk Asser wrote a biography of which "great" king of Wessex?

clues.clue: Later legend holds he burned a peasant woman's cakes while in hiding from the Danes, a story absent from Asser's original biography
examples: Asser's biography of this man doesn't mention him burning a peasant's cakes while on the run from the Danes.

clues.clue: Legendarily found an infant he named Nestingum in an eagle's nest, wearing gold armbands and wrapped in a purple cloak
examples: One account claims that this man found an infant he named Nestingum in an eagle's nest wearing gold armbands and wrapped in a purple cloak.

clues.clue: The Alfred Jewel, a gold-encased enamel and rock-crystal jewel discovered near Athelney, is held in the Ashmolean Museum
examples: The Ashmolean Museum contains a gold-enclosed enamel and rock crystal jewel named for this ruler that was discovered near Athelney.

clues.clue: The Leechbook of Bald records remedies used by one Elias of Jerusalem to treat him, leading some to suspect he suffered from Crohn's disease
examples: The Leechbook of Bald offers insight into remedies used by Elias of Jerusalem to treat this ruler, who likely suffered from Crohn's disease.

clues.clue: A letter of recommendation he wrote for a monk gave its name to the Grimbald Gospels
examples: A letter of recommendation written to this king for the eponymous monk provides the name of the Grimbald Gospels.

clues.clue: His wife (Ealhswith) founded Nunnaminster, a convent in Winchester, the city protected by his largest fortification
examples: This king's wife established Nunnaminster in a city protected by the largest of a series of fortifications across his lands; that city was Winchester.

comprehensive_summary: Alfred the Great (born c. 849) ruled Wessex from 871 to 899 and is the only English monarch ever given the epithet "the Great," traditionally described as meaning "wise elf." His main medieval biography, the Life of King Alfred, was written by the Welsh monk Bishop Asser, though later folklore added stories absent from that text, such as Alfred burning a peasant woman's cakes while in hiding from the Danes, and a legend of him finding an infant named Nestingum in an eagle's nest. Before becoming king, he fought at the Battle of Ashdown (871), allegedly rallying troops with the "Blowing Stone" and helping kill the Viking leader Bagsecg. His defining achievement came at the Battle of Edington (878), where he decisively defeated Guthrum's Viking army after hiding out at a fortress on the Isle of Athelney (near which the Alfred Jewel, now in the Ashmolean Museum, was later discovered). Edington produced the Treaty of Wedmore, negotiated with Guthrum: it established the Danelaw along a boundary running through Watling Street, required Guthrum's baptism under the name Aethelstan and adoption as Alfred's godson, divided Mercia between the two rulers, and set compensation for unlawful killing at eight half-marks of gold. To defend against further Viking incursions, Alfred built a network of fortified towns called burhs, documented in the Burghal Hidage, a system later extended by his daughter Aethelflaed, "Lady of the Mercians," and his son Edward the Elder, under whom Alfred became the first West Saxon king to style himself "King of the Anglo-Saxons." He also compiled the law code known as the Doom Book, building on the earlier codes of Aethelberht of Kent and Offa of Mercia, and the Leechbook of Bald records treatments given to him for an illness some identify as Crohn's disease. Alfred sponsored a program of Old English translations of texts he deemed "most necessary for all men to know," including Gregory the Great's Pastoral Care and Boethius's Consolation of Philosophy (translated, per his preface, "sometimes word for word, sometimes sense for sense"), and is credited with a translation of Orosius incorporating Ohthere's account of walrus hunting. He is linked to the compilation of the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle during his reign, to a translation of Aesop's Fables that Marie de France claimed as the source for her own Fables, and to a body of apocryphal wise sayings, the "Proverbs of Alfred," quoted in the Middle English poem The Owl and the Nightingale. A letter of recommendation he wrote for a monk gave its name to the Grimbald Gospels, and his wife, Ealhswith, founded the Nunnaminster convent in Winchester, the city protected by his largest fortification.
recursive_suggestions: Guthrum — Viking ruler defeated at Edington, baptized as Aethelstan under the Treaty of Wedmore; has independent tossup/bonus-part presence (fetched here only where Alfred is the actual answer)
Aethelflaed — Alfred's daughter, "Lady of the Mercians," has an independent bonus-part answerline (B10P2) not included here since it doesn't clue Alfred directly
Edward the Elder — Alfred's son and successor, has independent bonus-part presence (B4P2, B7P3)
Bishop Asser — Alfred's biographer, has an independent bonus-part answerline (B1P3)
Ivar the Boneless / Ragnar Lodbrok / Great Heathen Army — recurring Viking-era answerlines with independent bonus-part presence (B6P3, B11P2, B13P2)
Marie de France — medieval author who claimed to translate Alfred's Aesop; has her own independent bonus-part answerline (B3P1), a Literature topic
Hywel Dda / the Picts — surfaced only as unrelated bonus parts in a 'early medieval British leaders' bonus (B5) alongside Alfred; excluded here as different topics
Wessex / Mercia / Heptarchy — recurring Anglo-Saxon kingdom answerlines that appear constantly as context for Alfred's reign; likely worth their own topic pages
